text: The enormous chemical diversity of antioxidants in plants and other living organisms makes their isolation, identification and quantification in foods and biological systems highly challenging. Moreover, it is often more relevant to measure the total antioxidant capacity of a sample, food or living organism since their beneficial health-related activities are generally the result of their synergistic action. Due to the lack of generally accepted methods for the quantitative determination of antioxidants for food labelling purposes, food analyses presently rely on an array of experimental methods, which differ depending on whether they measure antioxidant activity based on an electron transfer (ET) or hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) mechanism. The individual measurements are however not highly inter-correlated because of the differences in redox potential, pH dependence, or requirement of different media and conditions. This project will therefore look at an in-depth characterisation of the antioxidant itself and at its mutual interactions and, by developing a more logical classification of the antioxidant assays with an account of their intrinsic limitations, with the aim of providing more cohesion and clarity in the present plethora of tests.
